question:
Find the smallest positive integer $j$ such that for every polynomial $p(x)$ with integer coefficients and for every integer $k$, the integer
\[
p^{(j)}(k) = \left. \frac{d^j}{dx^j} p(x) \right|_{x=k} 
\] 
(the $j$-th derivative of $p(x)$ at $k$) is divisible by 2016.

descriptive_long.question:
Find the smallest positive integer derivindex such that for every polynomial polynomial(varinput) with integer coefficients and for every integer integerk, the integer
\[
polynomial^{(\text{derivindex})}(integerk) = \left. \frac{d^{\text{derivindex}}}{d{varinput}^{\text{derivindex}}} polynomial(varinput) \right|_{varinput=integerk} 
\] 
(the derivindex-th derivative of polynomial(varinput) at integerk) is divisible by 2016.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Find the smallest positive integer $butterfly$ such that for every polynomial $notebook(pineapple)$ with integer coefficients and for every integer $bookshelf$, the integer
\[
notebook^{(butterfly)}(bookshelf) = \left. \frac{d^{butterfly}}{d pineapple^{butterfly}} notebook(pineapple) \right|_{pineapple=bookshelf} 
\]
(the $butterfly$-th derivative of $notebook(pineapple)$ at $bookshelf$) is divisible by 2016.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Find the smallest positive integer $integralindex$ such that for every polynomial $constantform(constantvalue)$ with integer coefficients and for every integer $fractionpoint$, the integer
\[
constantform^{(integralindex)}(fractionpoint) = \left. \frac{d^{integralindex}}{dconstantvalue^{integralindex}} constantform(constantvalue) \right|_{constantvalue=fractionpoint} 
\] 
(the $integralindex$-th derivative of $constantform(constantvalue)$ at $fractionpoint$) is divisible by 2016.

garbled_string.question:
Find the smallest positive integer $qzxwvtnp$ such that for every polynomial $twsrkmab(hjgrksla)$ with integer coefficients and for every integer $mprxvaet$, the integer
\[
 twsrkmab^{(qzxwvtnp)}(mprxvaet) = \left. \frac{d^{qzxwvtnp}}{d{hjgrksla}^{qzxwvtnp}} twsrkmab(hjgrksla) \right|_{hjgrksla=mprxvaet}
\]
(the $qzxwvtnp$-th derivative of $twsrkmab(hjgrksla)$ at $mprxvaet$) is divisible by 2016.

kernel_variant.question:
Determine the least positive integer $j$ with the following property:
for every polynomial $p(x)$ having integer coefficients and for every integer $k$, the number
\[
          p^{(j)}(k)=\left.\frac{d^{\,j}}{dx^{j}}\,p(x)\right|_{x=k}
\]
is divisible by $9450$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Write  9450 in its prime factorisation:
9450 = 2\cdot 3^3\cdot 5^2\cdot 7.

1. (A necessary condition.)
   Suppose j satisfies the stated divisibility condition.  Evaluate it on the specific polynomial
   q(x)= (x+1)^j.  Differentiating j times gives q^(j)(x)=j! for every x, so in particular j! must be divisible by 9450.

2. (Finding the smallest such j.)  We determine the minimal j for which 9450|j! by comparing prime-power exponents.
   * For the factor 7 we need j\geq 7.
   * For 3^3 we need \lfloor j/3\rfloor +\lfloor j/9\rfloor +\lfloor j/27\rfloor  \geq  3, forcing j\geq 9.
   * For 5^2 we need \lfloor j/5\rfloor +\lfloor j/25\rfloor  \geq  2, forcing j\geq 10.
   * The single factor 2 causes no further increase.
   Thus the least candidate is j=10, and indeed
     10! = 3 628 800 = 384 \times  9450,
   so 9450|10!.  Consequently every admissible j must satisfy j\geq 10.

3. (Sufficiency of j=10.)  Let p(x)=\sum _{m=0}^n a_m x^m (a_m\in \mathbb{Z}) and fix an integer k.  Repeated differentiation gives
   p^(10)(k)=\sum _{m=10}^n m(m-1)\cdots (m-9) a_m k^{m-10}
            =10! \sum _{m=10}^n (m choose 10) a_m k^{m-10}.
   The binomial coefficient (m choose 10), the coefficients a_m, and k^{m-10} are all integers, so the right-hand side is an integer multiple of 10!.  Because 10! itself is a multiple of 9450, the expression p^(10)(k) is divisible by 9450 for every p and k.

4. (Conclusion.)  The integer j=10 both is necessary and suffices, hence is minimal.

Boxed answer: j=10

core_steps: Use the test polynomial p(x)=x^j to force the condition N | j! (since p^{(j)}(k)=j!)
Compute the prime factorization of N to find the smallest j with N | j! (lower bound on j)
For that j, write the j-th derivative of a general integer-coefficient polynomial as p^{(j)}(k)=j!·Σ a_m·C(m,j)·k^{m-j}
Because a_m, k, C(m,j) are integers, j! divides every such derivative (upper bound)
Combine the bounds: that j is both necessary and sufficient, hence minimal

slot1.description: Modulus whose divisibility is demanded; replacing it by any positive integer leaves the argument intact

slot2.description: Specific polynomial used in the necessity step; any monomial of degree j works
